justification: All 4 business_impact sub-fields (financial, operational, user, risk) are substantively populated with multi-sentence analysis. stakeholders_affected lists 8 distinct groups with specific impact per group (far exceeds the 3 minimum). Multiple escalations for missing information (cutoff definition, tier scoping, business motivation, B2B contracts). Contractual SLA risk explicitly identified in both the risk field and a dedicated B2B escalation. confidence.level is 48, well under the 80 threshold, with detailed assumptions and low-confidence areas. Competitive positioning is mentioned in both the user impact and risk sections.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough stakeholder analysis with 8 distinct groups, each with specific impact descriptions — far beyond the minimum requirement
Confidence level of 48 is well-calibrated given the missing contract information and unstated business motivation
Four escalations covering cutoff ambiguity, tier scoping, business motivation, and B2B contracts — each with options and recommendations
Contractual SLA risk explicitly called out with a dedicated escalation recommending B2B contract review before proceeding
Competitive positioning clearly addressed — notes the industry trend toward tighter windows and frames date-only as moving against the grain
Financial analysis captures both the positive (artificial SLA improvement) and negative (premium tier revenue erosion, churn risk) dimensions
Edge cases are exceptionally strong — same-day delivery, signature-required, B2B dock hours, and cross-timezone scenarios all identified
Proactively suggests a middle-ground alternative (time-window ETAs like '2-4 PM Friday') rather than just critiquing the proposal
Sales/account management is not listed as a distinct stakeholder group in the structured list, though B2B account impact is covered under the B2B customer entry and escalation

trials.justification: All 6 pass criteria met. (1) All 4 business_impact sub-fields (financial, operational, user, risk) contain substantive multi-sentence analysis with specific details like WISMO cost estimates and competitive trend analysis. (2) stakeholders_affected lists 7 distinct groups (end consumers, B2B/enterprise, customer support, dispatch ops, integration partners, sales/account mgmt, product/marketing) each with specific impact descriptions. (3) Three formal escalations raised for missing context: on-time delivery rate, service tier scope, and API contract details. (4) Contractual SLA risk explicitly identified in BR-3, the risk sub-field, and a dedicated escalation. (5) confidence.level is 52, well within the ≤80 threshold, with 4 listed assumptions. (6) Competitive positioning addressed in both financial ('competitors showing precise ETAs appear more reliable') and risk ('logistics is trending toward MORE precision — Amazon, DoorDash').

observations.text: Confidence level of 52 is well-calibrated — reflects genuine uncertainty about contract details and on-time rates rather than defaulting to a safe middle number
Escalations are exceptionally well-structured with options and recommendations, going beyond the minimum requirement of flagging missing info
Identified the WISMO metric as a key cost driver and included it as an acceptance criterion (AC-6) with a concrete threshold — shows deep domain understanding
BR-3 and BR-4 correctly identify that SLA-bound and premium-tier shipments must be carved out, with testable assertions
Edge cases are strong — perishable goods, timezone boundary issues, in-flight shipment transitions, and same-day delivery all identified
Correctly frames the operational benefit (reduced last-mile pressure) while explicitly balancing it against customer experience cost and competitive risk
The 'Out for Delivery' narrowing edge (AC-5) shows nuanced thinking about when precision becomes safe to restore

trials.justification: All 4 business_impact sub-fields (financial, operational, user, risk) are populated with substantive, multi-sentence analysis. stakeholders_affected lists 8 distinct groups with specific impact per group (far exceeding the 3 minimum). Multiple escalations raise missing context that Bird cannot assume — including contractual SLA risk and business motivation. Contractual SLA risk is explicitly identified in both the risk field and a dedicated escalation. confidence.level is 45, well under the 80 threshold, with clearly listed assumptions. Competitive positioning is mentioned in both the user impact ('competitors offer 1-hour delivery windows') and risk field ('industry trend is toward more precise ETAs').

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough stakeholder analysis with 8 distinct groups, each with specific impact descriptions — goes well beyond the minimum requirement
Confidence level of 45 is well-calibrated given the missing contract and motivation information, with clear reasoning for the low confidence
Three distinct escalations covering business motivation, contractual/tier scoping, and timezone ambiguity — each with structured options and recommendations
Correctly identifies that date-only ETAs could mask operational inefficiency rather than solving root cause
Financial analysis captures both sides: short-term SLA penalty reduction vs premium customer churn and B2B contract risk
Identifies API breaking change risk and need for versioning — a technical business risk many would miss
Edge cases are practical and well-reasoned (timezone boundaries, in-flight deliveries, API consumers)
Business rules correctly distinguish invariants from policy decisions with clear justifications
